utterances: And do remember when…

If there's people out there, if they have been to (unintelligible), even if they're not anymore and still have the student IDs, I found I could use that at a lot of places at museums and things like that -almost anywhere from Finland to other places in Europe and stuff like that.
Oh, those gullible Europeans.
Yeah.
(Soundbite of laughter)
Although seniors don't usually get in for the senior admission price because we don't reciprocate when they come here.
And they make a very clear point to that.
It puts off a lot of Americans seniors.
Why does that French person get in at half price and not me?
Well, because…
Oh, yeah.
…French reciprocates with Germany and United States doesn't when it comes to senior discounts.
Katrina makes a very good point about getting off the beaten path.
Italy is notorious for being expensive, but most people's experience in Italy, as Neal mentioned with the $10 cup of coffee, is in places like Venice, France and Rome where all the tourists go.
If you can go to the rest of Italy, you'll find prices are substantially less expensive.
And of course, what we think of as Eastern Europe is now part of the E.U.
and its wide open.
And boy, talk about the new frontier for European tourism and a lot of fun.
You'll find that hotels across the Eastern Europe are almost the same in the West, but experiences after that are much, much cheaper - great classical concerts in Prague for $15 up to $50.
We went to meals where, I mean, you know, to be like a four-star restaurant here - five courses…
Yeah.
and spend, you know, $80 for two people, which - but here, it would be 3-$400.
You know, it's just…
Yeah.
…a really nice experience.
That's a real tough call.
That's a real tough call.
I certainly am in a position where I wish that, when my children come to a decision like that, that they could come to me.
But again, you know, it's a double-edged sword.
I know very well why I didn't share this information with mine, and I'm sure there's plenty that they're not telling me, and that's kind of how it is between parents and children sometimes.
And have any of them, certainly, read the Constitution lately?
I went - I was just in Prague, and of course Prague is quite touristy, so you got to be careful or they'll rip you off in the restaurants.
But you get in the train for an hour and a half, go east, to Olomoucs, the capital of Moravia which nobody knows about.
And I was so impressed that you'd go to a restaurant and you don't even look at the price.
You order what ever you want.
They respected us, and we got great food for peanuts.
And remember, the very best beer in Europe is in the Czech Republic and it cost a dollar for a mug.
You pay eight bucks in Ireland and 10 bucks for a beer in Scandinavia.
The best stuff is in the Czech Republic.
Yeah, there's a lot of better places than Prague now.
(Unintelligible) go further South or other places that are like it, but, you're right.
I mean, a lot of those cities have just become so touristy and overpriced.
Well, Prague is the gateway to Eastern Europe and understandably, it's got more tourism now I think than Salzburg, and it's come with a big price.
But Krakow, in the historic capital of Poland is considered the one of the new Pragues.
And of course, Croatia is very popular now.
I was just in Bosnia and most (unintelligible) just wonderful.
I've had so much fun, by the way, writing my blog and reporting on all of this.

And it's fun to go places that a little offbeat, whether it's Morocco or Bosnia or (unintelligible) as Katrina was talking about and get away from the tourist crowd and closer to the people, and there you'll find your dollar stretching two or three times as far as it would in the more famous touristy places in the West.
Katrina, thanks very much for the call.
That blog that Rick Steves mentioned, well, you can go read that at our blog site at talk@npr.org/blogofthenation.
Both Rick Steves and Rick Seaney, our guest earlier in this program, have been kind enough to blog for us.
Rick Steves, thanks very much for being with us today.
Great to be with you.
Thanks, Neal.
And Rick Steves joined us today from the studio in Edmonds, Washington.
This is TALK OF THE NATION from NPR News.
This is TALK OF THE NATION.
I'm Neal Conan in Washington.
Right now, time for our monthly visit with The Motley Fool.

When the economy is in the dumps, one of the first assets you're likely to look at is your portfolio, that 401(k) plan at work or the college fund you put into the markets, or if you have questions about what to do with your investments in a shaky economy, how to protect yourself, maybe even make a little extra cash from the falling dollar, about trends or philosophies of investment, our number is 800-989-8255.
E-mail talk@npr.org.
David Gardner is the co-founder of The Motley Fool and is in a studio at their headquarters in Virginia.
And, David, we also have to do a one year wrap of my fantasy portfolio today, too.
So nice to have you on the program as always.
Thank you, Neal.
Good to be here.
And we'll get to calls in the moment.

But first, our usual words of caution, neither David nor I will offer advice on whether to buy yourself any particular stock, we're talking about a fantasy portfolio with imaginary money not meant to be financial advice and will not be a recommendation for or against any investment.
And a good thing, too, because of that fantasy $10,000 we invested right now, I made $9,329.69.
Well, it hasn't been a great year, Neal, but you've got some things to celebrate.
Again, we have to go to back to you Apple Computer call of the year ago; I think you made it around, well, 85, 90 today.
Even though it's down substantially from its high, it's on 130, so you got that one right and you even convinced me because I recently recommended it myself.
I really have to say Apple is one of the few bright spots in the market today.
As you may have noticed, the company announced 10 million iPhones; they're promising still to ship the in 2008.
So you have had some highlights.
And the other big winner was ConocoPhillips, a well-managed company, we think, in an oil industry where prices are going through the roof.
But nevertheless, some real stinkers too.
For example, well, Tempur-Pedic, my most recent purchase, which is down 40 percent.
You know - and I've had some of those too, who hasn't?
Yes, that was, sort of a - that was a December move, I believe.
Yeah.

And now, here we about to approach March, hasn't even been three months, really, it seems unfair but then again there's so many good companies, and I think Tempur-Pedic remains one that have had that kind of a thrill ride these last three months.

So, anyway, next month what we're going to do is take - I'm going to take a look at my holdings and decide, well, maybe after a year, it might be time to move some of my fantasy portfolio into other stocks.
We'll talk about that next month.
In any case, we got loads of questions about the economy in these conditions.
And well, David, just today the stock market is down again, the most recent flash I saw shows it down, what, 90 points on the day.
So as this steady drumbeat of bad days on Wall Street comes in, it's hard to sustain your discipline, isn't it?
Well it is, and you know a year ago if we were talking about the subprime problems they were just about breaking at this point.
The news and some of those companies which are today out of business that were making those loans were just - it was all coming to light right around now.

As you know, that's spilled out into a bigger story about the real estate market overall, and it wasn't just small banks who were once loaning to people that probably shouldn't have been getting loans in the first place, but rather more broadly.
It started to affect lots of banks, and then there were some questions about hedge funds, which there always will be I think for the rest of our lives.
Yup.
And then today, Neal, it's about is this economy slipping into a recession.
And I guess my foolish, with the capital F, point about this is it's always funny how often that word is use because there's very little group understanding of what recession even is.
Neal, not to put you on the spot but do you know, right off the top of your head, what a recession is?
Well, as you said, it starts out as a fairly narrow issue.
It's not a question of whether the state can have a parental notification law; it's whether the law must make an exception for medical emergencies.
The New Hampshire law says something like there must be parental notification, except if an abortion is necessary to prevent the death of the young girl.
So the ACLU and Planned Parenthood sued and said, `What about a severe health emergency?
You don't want a situation where a doctor has a young girl who is bleeding or something like that and can't get an abortion.
A federal judge and the US Court of Appeals in Boston essentially agreed with that claim.

They said the Supreme Court has said many times in the past that a state can't stand in the way of a woman's decision to get an abortion when her life or health is at stake, and so those courts struck down the law entirely.
And so on the theory that the law addressed the life of the mother, but not necessarily her health.
That's right, that the law was missing an important ingredient that the law must have.
I should say there's sort of a secondary sort of abstract issue here that could prove quite important, and it sort of goes like this.
Suppose you're a judge and you're faced with that situation, this very situation.

Should you strike down the law entirely and wipe it off the books, or should you say, `Well, 99 percent of the young girls are not going to have a health emergency, and parental notification is OK in principle, maybe I should uphold the law and just say if any young girl has a problem, she should go to a judge and seek a waiver'?

The Supreme Court's pattern in these cases is to strike down laws across the books and say if it could unfairly, unconstitutionally hurt the rights of some women, then the law itself is unconstitutional on its face.

But Chief Justice Rehnquist and Justice Scalia, in particular, have said, `No, we ought to uphold the law and say if an individual person, if an individual woman has a particular complaint, we ought to decide that case on a case-by-case basis or a judge should decide it on a case-by-case basis.'

The reason I'm mentioning all this is because that's the issue the New Hampshire state attorneys raise, this sort of broader question about how to view abortion laws, and the Supreme Court granted their appeal.
Hmm.
So there's at least a possibility that they're taking this case to sort of rule more broadly that as a general matter, the laws should stand and these should become case-by-case challenges.
Well, as we mentioned earlier, the court has not heard an abortion case for five years now.
Why this case, why now?
Well, I think the five years is explained by the fact that they don't particularly want to take these very difficult regulatory cases.
They are somewhat minor questions.
They are very contentious.
The court is closely split on it.
So they have passed on a number of these regulatory cases, and the `Why now?

question, my best guess is that at least four of them are interested in this sort of abstract issue I was talking about, about whether we should strike the laws down on their face or whether we should apply a sort of individual case-by-case approach.

And if they adopt the latter, then it will change abortion litigation quite a bit, because, as I say, the pattern has been to this point--is to say if there's a flaw in the law, we strike down the whole law.
And you say at least four justices had to be interested in it, because that's how many it takes to accept a case.
